name: The Journey to Paradise

outcome.condemned: Heart devoured by Ammit, cease to exist
outcome.justified: Become Akh (blessed spirit), dwell in Field of Reeds

judgment.weighing: Heart weighed against feather of Ma'at

journey.death: Soul separates into Ka (life force) and Ba (personality)
journey.burial: Book of the Dead, amulets, provisions
journey.duat: Navigate 12 hours of night through underworld

description:
The ancient Egyptians developed the most elaborate afterlife beliefs in human history. Death was not an ending
                but a transformation—a dangerous journey through the Duat (underworld) requiring preparation, knowledge, magical
                protection, and moral worthiness. Those who succeeded became eternal spirits dwelling in paradise. Those who
                failed faced oblivion.

keyMyths.title: The Weighing of the Heart Ceremony
keyMyths.description: In the Hall of Ma'at, the deceased's heart was weighed against the feather of Ma'at (truth). If the heart was lighter, the deceased was deemed worthy and allowed to pass into the afterlife; if heavier, it was devoured by the monster Ammit, resulting in a second, final death.

keyMyths.title: Ra's Nightly Journey Through the Duat
keyMyths.description: Each night, the sun god Ra journeyed through the Duat, battling the serpent Apophis to ensure the sun would rise again. This journey symbolized the challenges and dangers faced in the afterlife and the constant struggle against chaos.

keyMyths.title: The Field of Reeds (Aaru)
keyMyths.description: The Field of Reeds, or Aaru, was the idealized paradise where the worthy dead lived eternally. It was a mirror image of the earthly world, but abundant and perfect, where the deceased continued their earthly pursuits.

keyMyths.title: The Role of Anubis
keyMyths.description: Anubis, the jackal-headed god, played a crucial role in the afterlife journey, guiding souls through the underworld, overseeing the weighing of the heart, and protecting the deceased from dangers along the way. He was the patron of embalming and funerary rites.
keyMyths.source: Various funerary texts and tomb paintings

keyMyths.title: Navigating the Twelve Divisions of the Duat
keyMyths.description: The Duat was divided into twelve regions, each representing an hour of the night, and each containing unique challenges and monsters. The deceased had to overcome these obstacles, using spells and knowledge from the Book of the Dead to navigate the underworld safely.

sources.name: The Egyptian Book of the Dead
sources.description: A collection of spells, prayers, and hymns intended to guide the deceased through the afterlife.
sources.title: The Egyptian Book of the Dead

sources.name: The Amduat
sources.description: An important funerary text describing Ra's journey through the twelve hours of the night, providing a map of the Duat.
sources.title: The Amduat

sources.name: The Book of Gates
sources.description: A funerary text detailing the divisions of the Duat and the gates Ra must pass through each night.
sources.title: The Book of Gates

sources.name: The Pyramid Texts
sources.description: The oldest collection of Egyptian religious texts, inscribed on the walls of pyramids, containing spells and rituals for the pharaoh's afterlife.
sources.title: The Pyramid Texts

sources.name: Hornung, Erik. *The Ancient Egyptian Books of the Afterlife*
sources.description: A comprehensive academic analysis of the major Egyptian funerary texts and their depiction of the afterlife.
sources.title: Hornung, Erik. *The Ancient Egyptian Books of the Afterlife*
